content.problemStatement:
JJ has an array AAA initially of length NNN. He can perform the following operation on AAA:

**1)** Pick any index iii (1≤i≤∣A∣)(1 \le i \le |A|)(1≤i≤∣A∣) such that Ai>1A_i \gt 1Ai​>1 \\
**2)** Select any two integers XXX and YYY such that X+Y=AiX + Y = A_iX+Y=Ai​ and X,Y≥1X, Y \ge 1X,Y≥1 \\
**3)** Replace AiA_iAi​ with XXX and YYY

Note that the length of the array increases by 111 after each operation.

For example, if A=[4,6,7,2]A = [4, 6, 7, 2]A=[4,6,7,2], he can select i=1i = 1i=1 and select X=1,Y=3X = 1, Y = 3X=1,Y=3 (since X+Y=A1X + Y = A_1X+Y=A1​). After the operation array becomes: [4‾,6,7,2]→[1‾,3‾,6,7,2][\underline{4}, 6, 7, 2] \rightarrow [\underline{1}, \underline{3}, 6, 7, 2][4​,6,7,2]→[1​,3​,6,7,2].

JJ wants to make AAA palindromic. Find the minimum number of operations to do so.

It is guaranteed that AAA can be converted to a palindromic array by using the above operation.

Note: An array is called a palindrome if it reads the same backwards and forwards, for e.g. [1,3,3,1][1, 3, 3, 1][1,3,3,1] and [6,2,6][6, 2, 6][6,2,6] are palindromic.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output the minimum number of operations to make AAA palindromic.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 200000
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains an integer N, the length of the array. Second line contains N space-separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the minimum number of split operations required to transform the array into a palindrome.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: split 4 into 1+3 (1 op) → [3,7,6,1,3]; split 7 into 3+4 (1 op) → [3,1,6,6,1,3]; palindrome. Total 2.
Test case 2: already a palindrome, 0 operations.
Test case 3: four splits are needed as shown in the statement.

solution.approach: Use two pointers i (start) and j (end). While i < j compare A[i] and A[j]. If they are equal, move both pointers inward. If A[i] < A[j], split A[j] into A[i] and (A[j]-A[i]); this uses one operation, matches the left element, and we move i forward while reducing A[j]. Symmetrically, if A[i] > A[j] we split A[i] into A[j] and (A[i]-A[j]), count one operation and move j backward. Each split reduces the larger side to the smaller side, guaranteeing progress. The greedy choice is optimal because any solution must at least split the larger side to match the smaller one; doing it immediately never increases the number of operations.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.

code.Python3:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline
T = int(input())
answers = []
for _ in range(T):
    N = int(input())
    A = list(map(int, input().split()))
    i, j = 0, N - 1
    ops = 0
    while i < j:
        if A[i] == A[j]:
            i += 1
            j -= 1
        elif A[i] < A[j]:
            ops += 1
            A[j] -= A[i]
            i += 1
        else:
            ops += 1
            A[i] -= A[j]
            j -= 1
    answers.append(str(ops))
print('\n'.join(answers))
